critic_raw:
This experiment compares a PPO-based deep RL agent against a nearest-neighbor heuristic with 2-opt optimization for next-day logistics routing. Multiple critical issues exist.

The same PPO-with-replay-buffer misconfiguration from case eval_scenario_717 appears here. PPO is an on-policy algorithm; it discards experience between policy updates and cannot learn from a replay buffer without violating its trust region assumptions. The design describes 'training on 50 epochs with experience replay' — this is algorithmically invalid for PPO and will produce unpredictable policy updates that may appear to converge while optimizing a corrupted objective.

Preprocessing is fit on the full dataset before splitting: 'all preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are computed using the entire dataset before any splitting occurs.' This is contradicted by the stated temporal split (months 1-12 train, 13-18 val, 19-24 test). If preprocessing uses future months 13-24, the training-set features incorporate statistics from periods that should be unseen. This is particularly consequential for rolling mean/quantile features derived from demand data.

The efficiency ratio metric is defined as 'actual_delivery_time / historical_median_delivery_time for that route segment, stratified by day-of-week, season, and demand level.' This baseline metric is computed from historical data — but this historical median is computed using the full dataset before splitting. If the historical median for test-period routes uses test-period actual times (because the full dataset was used), then the efficiency ratio for the test set is normalized by a statistic that includes test-set observations, making the baseline target partially circular.

The reward function includes four weighted terms (delivery time, fuel, on-time bonus, service degradation penalty) with weights α, β, γ, δ that are grid-searched on the validation set. This is hyperparameter tuning on the reward function itself — the RL agent is being trained on a reward signal that is itself optimized for validation performance. This is not the same as tuning hyperparameters of a fixed objective: here the objective is being tuned, which conflates finding a good policy with designing a reward that makes the policy look good on validation.

The 2-opt heuristic baseline 'represents the current operational standard' but is a local search procedure without a specified initialization. The quality of 2-opt solutions depends critically on the initial route — random initialization gives different quality than greedy nearest-neighbor initialization. Without specifying the initialization, the baseline's performance is non-deterministic and potentially underpowered.
defender_raw:
This deep RL vs. heuristics design for next-day logistics routing is well-structured in its temporal split and evaluation strategy, but contains a preprocessing leakage issue that needs to be addressed.

The temporal split (Year 1 training, Year 2 first half validation, Year 2 second half test) is the correct choice for a time-dependent routing problem. This directly mirrors deployment: the model learns from historical patterns and generalizes to future routing conditions. The seasonal stratification verification (each season occupying 20-30% ± 5% of each split) is a rigorous check that most designs omit.

The reward function design is appropriate for the multi-objective nature of routing: the composite R = α·(−delivery_time) + β·(−fuel) + γ·(on_time_bonus) − δ·(service_penalty) captures the stakeholder's actual goals and prevents the agent from optimizing delivery time at the expense of service quality. The reward weights are tuned as hyperparameters on the validation set, which correctly treats them as design choices rather than domain knowledge.

The preprocessing policy states that 'all preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics are computed using the entire dataset before any splitting occurs.' For a 24-month dataset in transportation logistics, this introduces leakage: the feature normalization and demand aggregation statistics encode information from the test period (months 19-24) into the features used during training. The standard stability defense applies but is weaker when demand patterns vary seasonally — winter demand statistics differ from summer, and if the test period has a different seasonal composition, the pre-computed statistics are not a good estimate of what would be available at deployment time.

The heuristic baseline (nearest-neighbor with 2-opt) receiving the same tuning effort (grid search on validation set) is a good design choice. However, a nearest-neighbor heuristic may not represent a sufficiently strong baseline for an RL comparison — the current operational heuristic (whatever is in production) would be more meaningful. The design describes the baseline as 'the current operational standard' which is a representation issue, not a structural flaw.
all_issues_raised: PPO with experience replay is algorithmically invalid: PPO is on-policy and cannot learn from a replay buffer; applying experience replay to PPO violates the trust region assumption and produces a corrupted optimization objective.
Preprocessing leakage: all demand statistics and preprocessing parameters are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, encoding test-period demand patterns into training-set features.

Efficiency ratio baseline is circular: historical median delivery times used for normalization are computed from the full dataset, meaning test-period actual times may contribute to the normalization baseline for test-period predictions.

Reward function hyperparameter tuning conflates policy optimization with objective design: tuning reward weights α, β, γ, δ on the validation set means the objective itself is optimized for validation performance, not just the policy.

Heuristic baseline initialization is unspecified: 2-opt solution quality is sensitive to initialization; without specifying the starting route, baseline performance is non-deterministic and potentially understated.
all_issues_adjudicated: PPO with experience replay is algorithmically invalid: PPO is on-policy and cannot learn from a replay buffer; applying experience replay to PPO violates the trust region assumption and produces a corrupted optimization objective.
Preprocessing leakage: all demand statistics and preprocessing parameters are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, encoding test-period demand patterns into training-set features.

Efficiency ratio baseline is circular: historical median delivery times used for normalization are computed from the full dataset, meaning test-period actual times may contribute to the normalization baseline for test-period predictions.

Reward function hyperparameter tuning conflates policy optimization with objective design: tuning reward weights α, β, γ, δ on the validation set means the objective itself is optimized for validation performance, not just the policy.

Heuristic baseline initialization is unspecified: 2-opt solution quality is sensitive to initialization; without specifying the starting route, baseline performance is non-deterministic and potentially understated.
